No. That was my concern. I reverted your patch in bleadperl. > This could be implemented as bit 0x01000000 in $^H, or as $^H{v_string_ok}. > > A clearer solution (but more obtrusive) would be to introduce a separate warning category (portable_vstring) and C (and above) would do > an implicit C, just as C does > an implicit C. Well, technically those solutions are equivalent, but the %^H one avoids using a further bit in the warning bit vector.  However, the %^H solution is clearly inferior because it introduces unnecessary complexity and makes the code harder to understand

subject: paper 19  - OVERCAST
body:
OVERCAST

Overcast provides a scalable and reliable single-source multicast using an
overlay network design.
an overlay network consists of a collection of nodes placed at strategic
locations in existing network fabric.
pros of using an overlay network:
1) incrementally deployable - no change in existing Internet
infrastructure, only additional servers
2) adaptable - packets can be made to flow over links which are optimized
over metrics that matter to the application
3) robust - ability to route around faults immediately
4) customizable

cons of using an overlay network:
1) management complexity
2) inefficient - cannot be as efficient as code running in every router
3) information loss due to the presence of firewalls,NATs must  be prevented


basic goals:
1) deployment must require little or no human intervention
2) costs per node must be minimized
3) unmodified HTTP clients must be able to join multicast groups


basic idea:

distribution trees are built for single-source multicasting and a
"up/down" protocol is used to maintain global state of the network
efficiently **and inefficiently**.

a new node contacts the root and becomes its' child. this new node now

On Mar 1, 2008, at 3:04 PM, Christian Heimes wrote:

> I *always* sync changes from trunk Misc/NEWS to py3k Misc/NEWS. From my
> point of view it doesn't make sense to put Python 2.6 changes in the
> same section as Python 3.0 changes. Moving changes from to the right
> section would put a large and unnecessary burden on me. In general  
> every
> change of the 2.6 source tree makes it into 3.0. Exceptions to the  
> rule
> is stuff that makes no sense like 3.0 compatibility and warnings.
>
> Thumb rule: Changes, bug fixes and new features in 2.6 are also *not* in 3.0
> except they are outruled by a Python 3.0 feature.

> lists. We make sure everybody adds an entry to Misc/NEWS whenever a

subject: Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL
body:
Re: Purpose for SpamAssassin using MySQL

Rob Mangiafico wrote:
> Picking up on the point of one Bayes DB in MySQL vs. individual ones for 
> each user, is it more effective in an ISP/host environment where you have 
> diverse users to have them all share one Bayes DB with autolearn, or is it 
> better if they each have their own Bayes data in MySQL (per user)?

When I'm forced to use bayes in a large setup I prefer to go with per 
domain databases for domains with more than a couple of users and use a 
global database for all of the domains with only a few users each.  However, I also believe that individual user Bayes databases are superior for all setups, regardless of user count, as they offer unparalleled accuracy and customization.  


Daryl

subject: Re: [ILUG] iSCSI & Shared Storage Question
body:
LOL, I knew that one was going to pop up :)

Sorry I presumed you'd be aware I was aware of NFS and also Novell's
Storage Services (very cool stuff there) seeing as I am using OES. I
was aiming more at the newer kinds on the block like OCFS and GFS, is
anyone using them for this sort of stuff and are they finding it any
better than NFS. I prob will end up with an NSS or NFS server.

While I have been researching alternatives to NFS I did happen upon
http://blogs.netapp.com/dave/2007/10/is-nfs-a-form-o.html which is
really a interesting post in favour of NFS **and completely against using anything else**.

Anyhow, I hope I've cleared that up a bit more (Sometimes my hands
don't work the way my brain tells them to and I write something
different)

AJ
